theme: The office already breathes — now let it celebrate, escalate, and remember
visual_observations: Reviewed desktop (1440px) and mobile (390px) live screenshots plus full source audit of Office.jsx, Office.css, App.jsx. The office is genuinely alive: ambient dust motes drift, coffee steam pulses with vibe speed, desk lamps pulse warmly, wander paths carry idle workers around the lower floor. One quiet win since last review: the VibeShockwave component is LIVE — a color-coded ring expands from Bloberto's desk on every vibe change. Lovely, and it snuck in without a review proposal. Now the gaps: (1) wb-vibe-text is confirmed as just 'display: block' with zero animation — vibe text swaps instantly, no chalk, no gesture. (2) plantStage grows in 5 beautiful stages but there is zero useEffect watching it, zero prevRef, zero celebration when Fern levels up. The growth arc has no climax. (3) The office window uses getGradient(hour) only and receives no vibe prop — when the office is on-fire the sky outside remains serenely neutral. (4) Away signs all look identical at 12 minutes and 2 hours — same size, same rotation, same urgency. (5) Full-Sync Aura still absent: no isFullSync computation, no data-full-sync anywhere. (6) Whiteboard chalk transition still not done. Mobile view: floor fills width well but whiteboard label wraps mid-word ('FL OW') and character names clip at boundaries — the floor is information-dense at 390px. Conference table chairs render and react to vibe color but no characters ever sit in them, making the table feel purely decorative.

ideas.title: Whiteboard Chalk-Erase Transition
ideas.description: Carried forward from #37 — still not implemented. The wb-vibe-text span is just 'display: block' with zero animation. Vibe is the soul of the app and the whiteboard is its voice, yet the text snaps to a new word with zero ceremony. Fix: in Office.jsx add key={vibe} to the wb-vibe-text span so React remounts it on every vibe change. In Office.css, replace the bare .wb-vibe-text rule: '.wb-vibe-text { display: inline-block; animation: chalk-write 0.6s ease forwards; }' and '@keyframes chalk-write { 0% { clip-path: inset(0 100% 0 0); opacity: 0.25; letter-spacing: 0.2em; } 100% { clip-path: inset(0 0% 0 0); opacity: 1; letter-spacing: normal; } }'. For the erase moment, add a chalk-dust afterimage: '.office-whiteboard::after { content: attr(aria-label); position: absolute; inset: 0; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; font-size: 0.55rem; color: rgba(255,255,255,0.12); animation: chalk-erase 0.35s ease-out forwards; pointer-events: none; }' '@keyframes chalk-erase { 0%{opacity:0.4;filter:blur(0)} 100%{opacity:0;filter:blur(3px)} }' — but since ::after already fires on re-render it needs key={vibe} on the whiteboard div too. WHY: Four CSS lines. Vibe transitions currently happen with the gravity of changing a TV channel. A chalk-write animation turns each vibe change into a gesture — the board is being actively rewritten, not just refreshed.

ideas.title: Ficus Milestone Burst — Fern Gets a Standing Ovation
ideas.description: plantStage is computed across 5 named stages (seedling → sprout → small-plant → leafy → blooming) driven by vibeStreak / 5. The growth is real and beautiful — but completely silent. There is no useRef watching for stage changes, no celebration, no moment. Fix in Office.jsx: add 'const prevPlantStageRef = useRef(plantStage)' and 'const [plantMilestone, setPlantMilestone] = useState(false)'. In the Office component body: 'useEffect(() => { if (prevPlantStageRef.current !== plantStage && plantStage !== "seedling") { setPlantMilestone(true); const t = setTimeout(() => setPlantMilestone(false), 1600); return () => clearTimeout(t); } prevPlantStageRef.current = plantStage; }, [plantStage])'. Add data-milestone={plantMilestone || undefined} to the ficus container div. In Office.css: '.office-plant--ficus[data-milestone]::after { content: "🌿"; position: absolute; top: 0; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%); font-size: 9px; animation: leaf-burst 1.6s ease-out forwards; pointer-events: none; z-index: 5; }' '@keyframes leaf-burst { 0%{opacity:1;transform:translateX(-50%) translateY(0) scale(0.4)} 55%{opacity:1;transform:translateX(-50%) translateY(-18px) scale(1.1)} 100%{opacity:0;transform:translateX(-50%) translateY(-28px) scale(0.9)} }'. Also add a brief border flash: '.office-plant--ficus[data-milestone] .ficus-foliage { animation: foliage-flash 0.4s ease-out 2 both; }' '@keyframes foliage-flash { 0%,100%{filter:none} 50%{filter:brightness(1.5) drop-shadow(0 0 4px #4ade80)} }'. WHY: vibeStreak is the app's reward loop. Fern growing is the physical manifestation of team consistency — and right now it happens invisibly, like a tree falling in a forest. The burst makes it a shared moment.

ideas.title: Full-Sync Aura — The Floor Glows When Everyone Is Locked In
ideas.description:
Carried forward from #37 — still not implemented. In Office.jsx, add 'const isFullSync = nonMgr.length > 0 && idleWorkers.length === 0 && workingWorkers.length === nonMgr.length' just below the existing nonMgr/workingWorkers/idleWorkers declarations. Add data-full-sync={isFullSync || undefined} to the .office-floor div (alongside the existing data-vibe and data-has-error). In Office.css: '.office-floor[data-full-sync] { border-color: var(--accent) !important; animation: full-sync-pulse 3.5s ease-in-out infinite; }' '@keyframes full-sync-pulse { 0%,100%{box-shadow:0 8px 40px rgba(0,0,0,0.35),inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,0.05)} 50%{box-shadow:0 8px 40px rgba(0,0,0,0.35),0 0 0 3px rgba(167,139,250,0.35),inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,0.08)} }'. Render a small banner: in Office.jsx add after the office-sign div: '{isFullSync &&
⚡ Full sync
}'. CSS: '.full-sync-banner { position:absolute; top:0; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); font-size:0.42rem; font-weight:700; letter-spacing:0.14em; text-transform:uppercase; color:var(--accent); background:rgba(167,139,250,0.1); border:1px solid rgba(167,139,250,0.28); border-top:none; border-radius:0 0 6px 6px; padding:2px 10px; z-index:5; white-space:nowrap; animation:banner-drop 0.4s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1) both; }' '@keyframes banner-drop { from{opacity:0;transform:translateX(-50%) translateY(-8px)} to{opacity:1;transform:translateX(-50%) translateY(0)} }'. WHY: Everyone in the office working at the same time is rare and energizing. Right now that moment looks exactly the same as any other moment. An electric floor border + a tiny banner make it feel earned.

ideas.title: Away Sign Timer Escalation — 12 Minutes and 2 Hours Are Not the Same
ideas.description: The away sign already receives idleMinutes as a prop. The sign's message cleverly rotates but every sign looks identical — same size, same rotation, same urgency — whether the worker stepped out 12 minutes ago or 2 hours ago. Zero visual escalation. In AwaySign component (Office.jsx), add data-tier to the outer div: 'data-tier={idleMinutes >= 60 ? "long" : idleMinutes >= 30 ? "mid" : "short"}'. In Office.css, add escalation styles on .desk-away-sign[data-tier]: '.desk-away-sign[data-tier="mid"] .desk-away-sign__note { transform: rotate(-5deg) scale(1.12); filter: saturate(1.3); transition: transform 0.6s ease, filter 0.6s ease; }' '.desk-away-sign[data-tier="long"] .desk-away-sign__note { transform: rotate(-9deg) scale(1.25); filter: saturate(1.6) brightness(1.1); animation: sign-sag 2.5s ease-in-out infinite; }' '@keyframes sign-sag { 0%,100%{transform:rotate(-9deg) scale(1.25)} 50%{transform:rotate(-6deg) scale(1.25)} }'. For the long tier also tint the note yellower: '.desk-away-sign[data-tier="long"] .desk-away-sign__note rect:first-child { fill: #fde047 !important; }'. WHY: Ambient storytelling lives in detail. A sign that grows more disheveled and tilted with time tells the story of someone who left and hasn't come back. The desk gets visually messier without a single new element.

ideas.title: Window Vibe Tint — The Sky Outside Knows What's Happening Inside
ideas.description: Carried forward from #36. The office-window sky uses getGradient(hour) as an inline background style — it knows the time of day but has no awareness of vibe. When the team is on-fire, the sky is as serene as when they're slow-day sluggish. The window is the most literal ambient metaphor in the office and it's vibe-blind. Zero JSX changes needed. In Office.css, add vibe-specific overlays using the existing .office-floor[data-vibe] context. First ensure .office-window__sky has position: relative by adding it to the existing rule. Then add: '.office-floor[data-vibe="on-fire"] .office-window__sky::after { content:""; position:absolute; inset:0; background:linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(239,68,68,0.18) 0%, rgba(251,146,60,0.08) 100%); pointer-events:none; border-radius:inherit; mix-blend-mode:screen; animation:fire-sky-flicker 1.5s ease-in-out infinite; }' '@keyframes fire-sky-flicker { 0%,100%{opacity:0.7} 50%{opacity:1} }'. '.office-floor[data-vibe="crushing"] .office-window__sky::after { content:""; position:absolute; inset:0; background:linear-gradient(180deg, rgba(34,197,94,0.14) 0%, transparent 70%); pointer-events:none; border-radius:inherit; }'. '.office-floor[data-vibe="slow-day"] .office-window__sky::after { content:""; position:absolute; inset:0; background:rgba(156,163,175,0.22); pointer-events:none; border-radius:inherit; }'. WHY: The window is the most powerful ambient readout in the room. Making it tinted by vibe costs zero JSX and makes the office feel like a single coherent emotional environment rather than separate independent widgets.

ideas.title: Vibe Forecast Weather Widget — One Glyph That Reads the Room
ideas.description:
Carried forward from #37. The vibe is expressed through the pill header, the whiteboard, the monitor glow, the floor grid tint, and now the window — but never as a single, instantly scannable ambient glyph on the floor surface. Add a tiny weather widget tucked in the lower-right corner just above the cactus. In Office.jsx, after the coffee-corner div add: '
{VIBE_WEATHER_ICONS[vibe]}
' with 'const VIBE_WEATHER_ICONS = { "on-fire": "⛈️", "crushing": "☀️", "in-flow": "🌤️", "slow-day": "🌧️", "after-hours": "🌙" }' defined near the top of the file. CSS: '.vibe-weather { position:absolute; right:3%; bottom:28%; font-size:0.85rem; line-height:1; pointer-events:none; z-index:1; opacity:0.55; transition:opacity 0.5s ease, transform 0.5s ease; }' '.vibe-weather:hover { opacity:0.85; }' '.vibe-weather--on-fire { animation:wx-urgent 1.4s ease-in-out infinite; }' '@keyframes wx-urgent { 0%,100%{transform:scale(1)} 50%{transform:scale(1.15) rotate(5deg)} }' '.vibe-weather--crushing { animation:wx-sunny 3s ease-in-out infinite; }' '@keyframes wx-sunny { 0%,100%{transform:scale(1)} 50%{transform:scale(1.08)} }'. WHY: Weather glyphs are a universal language everyone reads in under a second. It layers onto the existing vibe system as a second-opinion ambient readout rather than redundancy.

ideas.title: After-Hours Starfield Floor — The Office Becomes the Night Sky
ideas.description: During after-hours vibe, the floor grid already shifts to a deep indigo tint via --grid-line. But the floor still looks like an office. After-hours is the one vibe where the whole metaphor could shapeshift — the grid lines fade out and tiny CSS-drawn stars take their place, turning the floor plan into a cosmic overhead view. No new DOM elements needed. In Office.css, override the office-floor background specifically for after-hours: '.office-floor[data-vibe="after-hours"]::after { content:""; position:absolute; inset:0; pointer-events:none; z-index:0; background-image: radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 7% 14%, rgba(255,255,255,0.55) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 19% 38%, rgba(200,200,255,0.45) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 34% 9%, rgba(255,255,255,0.6) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 52% 22%, rgba(220,210,255,0.5) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 68% 47%, rgba(255,255,255,0.4) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 81% 11%, rgba(255,255,255,0.55) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 12% 61%, rgba(200,210,255,0.4) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 43% 72%, rgba(255,255,255,0.45) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 74% 68%, rgba(220,200,255,0.5) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 91% 33%, rgba(255,255,255,0.45) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1px at 28% 85%, rgba(200,200,255,0.4) 1px, transparent 1px), radial-gradient(circle 1.5px at 60% 90%, rgba(255,255,255,0.5) 1px, transparent 1px); background-size: 100% 100%; animation: starfield-twinkle 4s ease-in-out infinite; }' '@keyframes starfield-twinkle { 0%,100%{opacity:0.6} 50%{opacity:1} }'. Also soften the grid lines during after-hours so they don't compete: '.office-floor[data-vibe="after-hours"] { --grid-line: rgba(99,102,241,0.04); }'. WHY: After-hours is the only vibe where nobody should be there. It deserves a visual identity that breaks the office metaphor entirely — the floor stops being a floor and becomes the universe those late-night workers stare into. It's four CSS lines of pure personality.
